blocks.content: The summer Millhaven almost burned down started with the three driest weeks in forty years, a fireworks ban that nobody respected, and Kit Jenkins deciding that Wednesday morning was as good a time as any to see how fast you could take the hill on Sycamore Street without braking.
Eli Turner had objected, briefly and unconvincingly, from the moment they'd pushed off the top of the hill to the moment they'd nearly taken out Mrs. Dupont's prize azaleas at the bottom.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: That was fine. We were fine.

blocks.speaker: Eli
blocks.content: You almost didn't have knees anymore.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: But I do. Currently. Still have them.

This was how it was with Kit Jenkins, and had been since approximately second grade. He'd arrive at an idea — usually a bad one — and present it with such unshakeable confidence that you found yourself going along with it right up until the moment it started going sideways, at which point he'd declare it fine, still going fine, probably going to be fine.

Eli, who had been best friends with Kit since they were seven and was old enough to have known better by now, had simply accepted this as a feature rather than a bug. You had more interesting summers if you ignored the part of your brain that was trying to prevent you from dying.

They picked up their bikes from the ditch. The morning was already thick with heat, the Georgia sun pushing through the oak canopy in long warm slants. Millhaven was the kind of town that smelled like cut grass and red clay until about ten a.m., when it switched to asphalt and honeysuckle. They were at the transition point.
Kit had already swung his leg over the seat.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: I'll know when we get there.
He pushed off down the street, and Eli followed, because that was how it went.
